rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Skills training and Technical Education for Employment Programme
rows.description:
There is a huge gap between the demand and supply of skilled man power in domestic market. The lack of employment opportunities, insufficient vocational skills training opportunities are pushing unemployed youths for out-migration. They get low remuneratio
n and are prone to accidents as they are mostly engaged in Danger, Difficult and Dirty (3D) jobs. Therefore, these unemployed rural youths need to be trained on emerging trades to sharpen their skills for their earnings and livelihoods. This will contribut
e to increased remunerations and job security. New opportunities have been waiting for the skilled youths within their own districts, regional markets, Indian markets as well as abroad markets.The project aims at enhancing access of women and youths to qua
lity vocational skill training and technical education by strengthening technical vocational education and training (TVET) sub-sector in Nepal. Expected results:• Enhanced access of women and youths to quality TVET service• TVET sub–sector knowledge 
and experiences are systematically documented and widely disseminated among the concerned• Strengthened capacity of public and private TVET service providers to deliver quality serviceRights-holders /beneficiaries: Unemployed women and youth Implementing
 partner: Underprivileged Children’s Education Programs (UCEP Nepal)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Strengthening Diversity to Promote Unity for Strong Trade Union Movement
rows.description:
This project will help to find out the current situation of CBAs and its implementations to support joint campaigning on issues like minimum wages and other issues for better industrial relation. It also helps to advocate and lobby for a worker friendly co
nstitution. At the same time, it will build the capacity of the 3 ITUC affiliates in key areas where they are weak in their organizations. The fundamental idea is to support the joint initiatives of the trade union movement in Nepal to promote permanent pe
ace from the workers side, while also simultaneously addressing the core weakness of the 3 national centers which play a key role in the JTUCC. Nepal trade union movement is fairly young and fragmented into 11 central unions, which are typically aligned wi
th political parties. In order to be effectively able to influence constitution drafting process, minimum wages setting and social security, the movement must act in cooperation on joint agenda. This is made possible through JTUCC and also each union’s c
apacities will be enhanced.The minimum wage setting happens in every second year in Nepal. This project will help trade unions make informed decisions and plans re minimum wage campaign and advocacy work on the same. Each union’s weaknesses will be enhan
ced through training.The beneficiaries are each central union, their officers and leaders, and in longer run all Nepalese workers.Implementing partners are central unions through their joint platform JTUCC, and the contracting partner will be ITUC NAC, as 
all three unions are ITUC affiliates.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Protection and Promotion of the Rights of Women Human Rights Defenders
rows.description:
In Nepalese society, women are subject to discrimination, and women human rights defenders statue is not sufficiently recognized, even in the Nepal's human rights movement. Before WOREC's 2006 campaign, women human rights defenders’ status, rights, and t
he need for protection in the first place had not been raised. The change has been made, but slowly, which is why women human rights defenders are still harassedin their work. Violence against female human rights defenders and even murders are not uncommon
. Perpetrators may be government officers, family members or other members of the community. WOREC has recently been involved in the drafting a legislative initiatives related safety of women human rights defenders. The project's main objective is to stren
gthen women human rights defenders in the capacity of human rights, particularly women's rights, promoting and improving the conditions for the work of human rights defenders. The primary target group of the project are especially women human rights defend
ers who are involved in WORECin networks and women's groups. In particular, the project takes into account the women who work for Dalits, indigenous, madhesien, the disabled, the Muslims and the victims of violence involved. The project capacitates other b
eneficiaries, such as community leaders and communities of men, as well as with national-level advocacy of women's rights. Women’s Rehabilitation Centre WOREC was founded in 1991, is registered as a national NGO, which is working with grassroots actors i
n Nepal, in order to remove the prevailing economic, social, cultural and human rights injustices and for the promotion of social justice and sustainable living conditions for grassroots communities. http://www.worecnepal.org/

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Nepal country programme
rows.description:
Nepal’s biodiversity is under constant threat from habitat degradation and fragmentation, deforestation and forest degradation which are driven by unplanned infrastructure development, uncontrolled forest fires. The iconic Himalayan species as tigers and
 snow leopards are threaten by poaching and illegal wildlife trade as well as causing human wildlife conflicts in some areas. Moreover, climate change has exacerbated the threats to biodiversity and people’s livelihoods.We work in two priority landscapes
 in Nepal: Terai Arc Landscape in Southern-Nepal and Sacred Himalayan Landscape in North-East-Nepal. The conservation work improves corridor and buffer zone conservation and management, reduces deforestation and strengthens community-based conservation. Ou
r work enables increase in the population of key species such as rhino and tiger by decreased illegal wildlife trade and poaching and protection of their habitats. Improvement of the livelihoods and wellbeing of the local communities is gained through deve
loping income generating activities such as forest-based enterprises and by increasing access to energy and developing health and hygiene solutions. In addition we increase conservation awareness and empower local youth to act for the environment. We also 
develop integrated river basin management and water smart communities which help local communities to adapt in climate change. Beneficiaries: the local community forest user groups, women and youth groups, ethnic minority groups, forest workers and disadva
ntaged groups. Implementor: WWF Nepal: http://www.wwfnepal.org/, More information about our interventions: http://www.wwfnepal.org/what_we_do/special_projects/decent_work__healthy_environment/expected_results/, Generation Green: http://tgg.org.np/, Buildin
g and Wood Worker's International - Nepal Affiliates Committee: https://www.facebook.com/bwi.nac

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Improving school environment in Nepal
rows.description:
In rural Nepal especially girls encounter challenges related to schooling, one of them being lack of toilets suitable for girls. There is also need for improvement in teachers' attitudes, transparency of school administration, infrastructure and safe envir
onment. With closer cooperation betweeen different school stakeholdersthe quality of schools can be improved and the attitudes in communities can be changed to understand the importance of schooling in children's life. The project aims to develop child and
 environmentally friendly schools in rural Eastern Nepal by supporting school child clubs in their activities and the cooperation between school stakeholders (child club, school management committee, parent-teacher assotiation, school administration, Villa
ge education committee) in preparing school improvement plans (SIP). The project provides capacitation to Child Club board members on child rights, life skills, gender, social inclusion and environmental issues. The school stakeholders participate in capac
itations and workshops to manage the schools efficiently and transparently. Through Child Clubs the youth can contribute to the development of their own shools and communities. There is also hardware components to improve the classrooms, sanitation and saf
e drinking water facilities in project schools. The project will result quality rural schools with good environment to study and work.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Rural Village Water Resources Management Project (III phase)
rows.description:
The Rural Village Water Resources Management Project in Nepal Phase II (2010-2015) has after four years of implementation achieved better water supply for 93 000 people sanitation for 269 000 people and home gardens for 155 000 people. The purpose of phase
 III is to achieve full coverage of water supply and sanitation and establishment of functional planning and implementation frameworks for all water uses in nine project districts. The expected results of the project are: 1) institutionalized community cap
acity to construct and maintain community managed water supply and adopt appropriate technologies and behavior 2) Improved and sustainable nutrition food security and sustainable income at community level through water resources based livelihoods developme
nt 3) Institutionalized capacity at district level to continue integrated water resources planning and support to communities in implementing and maintaining WASH and livelihood activities. In terms of beneficiaries the project will reach over 300 000 peop
le. The executing agency is Ministry of Federal Affairs and Local Development of Nepal together with participating District Development Committees (DDC). Finland will support the project with 15 million euros from 2015 to 2020.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Improving sexual and reproductive health and rights and biodiversity conservation through integrated approach
rows.description:
The development problem of the proposed project is limited access to SRHR combined with unsustainable use of natural resources. The purpose of the project is to improve SRHR and biodiversity conservation through integrated approach. The project focuses on 
peoples empowerment capacity building and awareness raising improving livelihood and well-being as well as strengthening partnerships to promote an integrated approach. Capacity building and awareness raising activities will be targeted towards young peopl
e and Natural Resource Management communities. Through the trainings it is expected that their understanding of the issues and motivation to promote sustainable development and SRHR are increased. The long-term development objective for the integrated Popu
lation Health and Environment (PHE) initiative is Healthy and productive communities where young people can live in harmony with nature and realize their SRHR. This project is a continuation project and forms a third phase of a collaborative PHE project wh
ich is implemented through an integrated approach with four partners: Väestöliitto (the Family Federation of Finland) FPAN (the Family Planning Association of Nepal) WWF Finland and WWF Nepal. Cooperation between these partners started already in 2011 an
d this project builds upon the lessons learned from the first two phases of the project. The project will be directly linked to the PHE initiative of WWF Nepal and WWF Finland supported by the MFA. These two projects will complement each other and there wi
ll be close partnership and coordination with WWF Nepal at the central and field levels.Capacity building and awareness raising activities will be targeted towards young people and Natural Resource Management (NRM) communities.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Preventive Education for Uterine Prolapse in Nepal
rows.description:
In Nepal there are about 14 million women with Uterine prolapse. Though the expected lifetime has become longer in Nepal the amount of women who get uterine prolapse has not decreased. It has doubled from the year 2008. The problem is in the Nepalese cultu
re. It does not protect the the women after they given a birth to a child. Many of the women are expected to work hard soon after the delivery.The aim of this project is to secure a better health for all in Nepal and to prevent Uterine prolapse- by support
ing them to appreciate the motherhood and to protect the mothers after the delivery - by counselling in breast-feeding and in child care- by teaching right ways to lift and to work - by guiding to healthy living habits and versatile diet- by encouraging to
 smoke free lifestyle with no intoxicating agents or fear of AIDSIt is planned that the project will reach all the 75 districts in Nepal in three years. Partners in the districts will be the health and education departments of the districts and also the vo
luntary women workers groups which already exist in the districts. According to the plan in each district a three day seminar will be arranged in which all the health teachers from the schools health assistants midwives and the voluntary women workers in t
he district will be invited. Each year about 2500 health workers can take part in the seminar (100 in each district). All these will get the health education teaching material used during the seminar so that they can use it also in their own work. So the a
mount of the Nepalese reached by the project will be several time larger.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UN Women - Women's Economic Empowerment
rows.description:
UN Women's Women's Economic Empowerment project aims to enhance the economic situation of especially the most poorest groups of Nepalese women. The participation of Nepalese women in the advancement of the national economy is limited due to insufficient ec
onomic resources and low level of education. The project advances the consciousness of the beneficiaries regarding their rights through networks of marginalized women. The project builds up the capacity of the Government of Nepal to follow the conditions o
f equality in its economic policy both at national and provincial level. Specific beneficiaries can be named female migrant workers domestic servants HIV positive women victims of conflicts and human trafficking and the female farmers of remote areas. UN W
omen's office in Nepal will execute the project and contract will be concluded with the main office of UN Women. Finland will support the project with 4 000 000 euros from 2015 to 2017.

rows.title: ODA equity through Finnfund
rows.description: Hydro projects

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Technical Assistance for Competence-Based Soft Skills Development in School Education in Nepal
rows.description:
The Technical Assistance for Competence-Based Soft Skills Development in School Education in Nepal (TASS) is the second phase of the Finnish funded TSSP program which was implemented in the years 2012-2015. The aim of the TSSP program was to improve the qu
ality of education in Nepal by integrating soft skills into teaching. Soft skills are defined as personal cooperation thinking innovation and business skills. The TSSP project ends in December 2015.The mid-term review of TSSP concluded that the integration
 of soft skills into the education sector in Nepal is highly relevant as it can improve both the quality and the relevance of education. The evaluation recommended a continuation of the project. The main achievements of TSSP were the approval of new curric
ula in six different subjects for grades 9-10 as well as the development of teachers training materials and their testing. The new textbooks for grade 9 will be in schools in 2016 and teachers' training packages have been taught in 40% of Nepal's teacher t
raining facilities. The second phase ? the TASS project ? will build on the achievements of the first phase. During the TASS program the update of the curriculum for grades 9-10 moves to the implementation stage. Teaching materials are being developed also
 for grades 6-8 and work on materials for grades 9-10 continues. The education of teachers and capacity building of relevant institutions regarding the application of soft skills also continues. The beneficiaries of the project are Nepalese students in gra
des 6-10. The immediate beneficiaries are the Ministry of Education and regional and district level education directorates and offices. In addition the projects 80 pilot schools and their teachers benefit from focused capacity building support. The Ministr
y of Education in Nepal implements the project. The proposed budget for the project is 17 MEUR for the years 2016-2018.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UNICEF - WASH Earthquake Recovery Programme
rows.description:
The catastrophic earthquake of 25th April 2015 measuring 7.8 Richter scale had its devastating consequences on human lives and property as well as infrastructures including WASH services in Nepal. The gains that Nepal had made so far in achieving the MDG t
argets on water and sanitation has been negatively challenged by the damages of water supply and sanitation services. UNICEF WASH Earthquake Recovery Programme will support reconstruction efforts in Nepal particularly in WASH sector with a particular focus
 on Disaster Risk Management. The expected results and outcomes of the programme are formulation and operationalization of disaster risk reduction and climate change policies capacity building and system building for disaster preparedness and response and 
earthquake affected water supply schemes and school toilets and water systems rehabilitation. Rehabilitated water supply schemes will serve at least 33 000 people and school toilets and water systems rebuilding will serve at least 20 000 children. At least
 20 000 school children will be also educated on hand washing.In International Conference on Nepal?s Reconstruction on 25th of June 2015 Finland pledged two million euros (2 000 000) to the reconstruction efforts in Nepal. The contribution was decided to b
e channeled through UNICEF based on earlier cooperation. Finland provided one million euros (1 000 000) support for 2016. The programme will receive additional funding of one million euros for 2017 as pledged in 25th of June 2015.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Support to Nepal's School Sector Development Plan (SSDP)
rows.description:
The School Sector Development Plan (SSDP) program covers the entire basic education system in Nepal and is funded jointly by the Government of Nepal and its Development Partners. The program is implemented during the years 2016-2023. Finland supports the p
rogram with a total of 20 million euros in the years 2016-2020. In addition Finland also supports a smaller technical assistance based project which supports the achievement of SSDP's objectives. The SSDP is the second phase of the School Sector Reform Pro
gramme which Finland supported between 2009-2016. It is based on the achievements of the first stage of which the most important were the substantial increase in access to education achievement of gender parity in education a decrease in illiteracy and cla
ssroom size and an increase in the number of educated teachers. In the SSDP there the focus shifts more clearly towards enhancing the quality equitable participation and learning outcomes in education. There is also a stronger emphasis on linking disbursem
ents of funds to achieved targets as donors will apply Disbursement Linked Indicators (DLIs) to which part or all of their funding is tied. The objectives of the program are divided into four categories: equity quality efficiency and relevance. The program
 aims to enhance these aspects of education on all levels: early childhood education basic education secondary education and lifelong learning. The program strives to ensure that all students in Nepal have equal opportunities to access high quality educati
on. A reform of the governance and management system of the education sector is also part of the objectives of the program. The final beneficiaries of the program are Nepal's around 7.3 million students and almost 29.000 public schools. The Ministry of Edu
cation of Nepal is implementing the program and the Government of Nepal funds at least 80% of its budget. Other development partners who fund the program include the Asian Development Bank Australia the EU the World Bank Norway UNICEF and Global Partnershi
p for Education.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Development Aid to Victims of Gorkha Earthquake among Persons with Disabilities in Rasuwa
rows.description: Support to disabled victims of Nepal earthquake, for building temporary shelters.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Self-employment for Persons with Disabilities victimized by earthquake
rows.description: A group of persons with disabilities want to establish a small mobile shop where they sell momo and soup.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empowerment programme for persons with disabilities (EPPD)
rows.description:
This project is about vocational training and income generating. The group will train 10 PWDs in bicycle repairing. The group will provide 20 other PWDs the opportunity to participate different types of vocational/income generating trainings. These people 
will then go back to their villages and utilize local government funds to arrange vocational trainings.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Ensuring safety and gender equality of blind and disabled women from cricket ground to the work places
rows.description:
The project is about gender equality. Cricket Association of the Blind CAB wants to raise awareness of gender equality to stop the harassment of blind women cricket players, and disabled women in general. CAB will organize two sexual awareness conferences 
and a mixed sexes cricket game.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empowering women with disability through skill development training and employment opportunity
rows.description: Twenty women with disabilities will be taught handicrafts for six months. The products will be showcased by establishing a small shop in Thamel area in Kathmandu. The shop will be rented for six months. Thamel is a well known market area.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Establishing the Rights of Marginalized Indigenous Tamang Children through Culturally Sensitive Learning
rows.description:
There are several ethnic groups and indigenous people in Nepal, and the country has internationally committed to improve the rights of indigenous people. The tamang language is part of Tibetan Burman languages, and it’s among the five most spoken languag
es in Nepal. Implementing linguistic rights has been a huge challenge for Nepal, in which over 100 languages are spoken and in which structural reforms are still ongoing. Even though time has been reserved for education of both Nepalese and a local languag
e, English is often taught instead of a local language due to lack of linguistic knowledge and negative attitudes. The project continues directly from a project previously funded by KIOS. The aim is to promote right to education on one’s mother tongue am
ongst children speaking Tamang language. Also the continuation of the project seeks to enhance tamang-speaking children’s cultural and educational rights in schools as well as inform about linguistic and cultural rights to the larger public. Childspace F
oundation Nepal was founded in 2003 by democracy and human rights activists belonging in tamang linguistic minority and indigenous population. The organization was originally registered as Helping Society Nepal in 2003, but the name was changed into Childs
pace Foundation Nepla in 2011. The organisation’s activities focus especially on improving the status and rights of tamang minority and, on a larger scale, on enhancing the rights of indigenous people and ethnic minorities. www.childspacefoundation.org

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Actions for Advancing Freedom of Freed Haliyas, Kamaiyas and Kamlaharis
rows.description:
Freed Haliyas, ex-Kamaiyas and Kamlaris (HaKK) have not yet fully realised the true sense of freedom. They are aware but still lacking desired level of empowerment. Thousands of right-holders are still denied their rights and entitlements. Therefore, right
-holders need to be further supported to empower them to claim and enjoy their rights. The duty-bearers require enough support in formulation policies, strategies, guidelines and implement those aiming the just rehabilitation of freed HaKK. The project, th
us, focuses on livelihood, education, capacity building and advocacy to facilitate duty-bearers’ and right-holders’ efforts to end this bondage system permanently. The overall goal of the project is to enhance the dignified living of freed Haliyas, Ex-
 Kamaiyas and Kamlaris. The project aims at advancing socio-economic condition of freed Haliya, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris enhancing their access to livelihood assets and promoting equality and non-discrimination Expected results:• Increased access of the
 rights-holders (i.e. freed Haliyas, ex-Kamaiyas and Kamlaris), their groups and associations to services and resources, and fulfillment of their rights • Enhanced access of the rights-holders to resilient livelihood and employment opportunities • Incr
eased access of children and youth among the rights-holders to uninterrupted quality education • Improved harmony and relations between freed bonded labourers and their ex-mastersRights-holders /beneficiaries: Freed Haliyas, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris.Imp
lementing partner: The Lutheran World Federation Nepal (LWF Nepal)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Equality and Non-Discrimination for Sustainable Peace (ENDS)
rows.description:
Dalits continue to be among the poorest, under educated and under-represented at the political level. The project will raise awareness on unlawful and inhuman practices of caste-based discrimination and untouchability among both Dalit and non-Dalit populat
ion as well as prepare them to speak up against these practices; educate and sensitise government authorities on the anti-discrimination legal provisions and measures. The project will mobilise human rights defenders for supporting victims and empower them
 to seek justice through formal process, and educate and empower women and youths to engage in peacebuilding process in their community. This will contribute for the protection and promotion of fundamental human rights and freedom of Dalit, women and other
 marginalised groups in Nepal.Expected results:• Enhanced voices and roles of Dalit and civil society organisations in encouraging government to implement non-discriminatory policies and equality frameworks contributing to peace• Enhanced participation
 and role of marginalised women and youths in peace building in the community with their increased access and linkage to educational and livelihood opportunities• Increased engagement of religious, social and political leaders in peacebuilding and social
 harmony dealing with injustice and discriminationRights-holders /beneficiaries: Dalit women and youth Implementing partner: Nepal National Dalit Social Welfare Organization (NNDSWO)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Grassroots Dialogues and Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction
rows.description:
Program I 1. Grassroots and National level dialogues will be organised under the Social Forum banner. SADED, through its grassroots dialogue, collects memories, thoughts and experience from the grassroots level 2. Those interesting and important memories a
nd outcomes of the dialogues will be published and disseminated through newsletters, newspaper articles, blogs and websites. 3. SADED-Nepal prioritizes to interact with various social movements in South Asia. Since it is easy for Nepalese to visit Pakistan
, and Bangladesh in comparison to Indians, SADED-Nepal will engage with social movements there so that regional social movements especially Social Forum will be strengthened. Program II: Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction (after 2015 earthquake)
 1. To stay with our green principles to use local technological skills and material for reconstruction: There is a risk that some of the multinational companies will monopolize the reconstruction discourse in order to expand their capital. SADED-Nepal has
 a plan to recruit some of the technicians to mobilize in the affected areas before and during the reconstruction work. They will be given chance to have exposure visits to places like Uttarakhand and Assam of India. These technicians will be able to sugge
st a good engineered structures so that the reconstructed houses with local materials will be equally or even more stronger than that of corporate promoted #modern# houses. Dialogues will be organized in the affected areas with the participation of communi
ty-based organizations and local elites. Also we will invite experts from Uttarakhand and Assam for sharing dialogues in the affected areas. 2. Resuming/Building/supporting public spaces for children and elders in the villages: SADED-Nepal have identified 
5 Village Development Communities (VDCs) in Nuwakot and Rasuwa/Sindupalchowk from where we start relief and reconstruction work of children's space. While doing so, pre-primary schools, play schools along with the village based public spaces will be recons
tructed where it is destroyed. We will take a VDC, and after having need assessments in the villages, will help reconstruct damaged infrastructures so that public spaces can resume/start their activities. Elderly and young people will be involved in runnin
g these children spaces. Furthermore, midday meals and play materials will be provided in a school and a children’s play space.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Accountable and Inclusive Schools
rows.description:
Inclusive and Accountable Schools is a cooperation project of Loo Niva Child Concern Patan Community Based Rehabilitation and Interpedia Finland Ry. The project running from 2017 to 2020 works in South Lalitpur which suffers from a low rate of public schoo
ls and was hard hit by the earthquakes in the spring 2015. This project builds on previous co-operation between Loo Niva and Interpedia with a new partnership with Patan CBR adding expertise in children with disabilities.Previous work aimed to contribute t
o strengthening efficient and effective school and local education governance mechanism in South Lalitpur. This project is focused on promoting inclusive and accountable schools as well as advancing the realization of the right to education for marginalize
d children who face discrimination particularly due to disabilities or the caste but also due to gender and ethnicity. The goal to increase inclusion of these vulnerable groups of children will be achieved by identifying and finding excluded children; prov
iding material and financial support that sustains enrolment to school; sensitizing training and rising awareness about rights needs and potential of marginalized children among children parents and teachers; improving school governance and using local med
ia for raising awareness of the importance of education for all children. The three organizations involved have versatile expertise of both advancing the right to education improving governance and advancing the involvement of children from margin groups f
or instance children with disabilities and dalit children. Moreover the project has been designed with consultation of children local communities and authorities for ensuring engagement of the local communities as well as the most effective implementation 
of the project in promoting the right to education. From the beginning the project has been planned in collaboration with local and national level authorities other NGOs and community groups for ensuring the sustainability and continuity of the good result
s of the project.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Unlocking doors to education for children with disabilities related to hearing and vision in Bara district Nepal
rows.description:
In Nepal children are not screened for visual or hearing impairments. Knowledge of eye- and ear health is generally poor. Traditional beliefs and treatments can be detrimental to sight and vision. Having a disability is still greatly stigmatised. Although 
the majority of children in Nepal have access to education differently abled children are still mostly excluded from it. This is a major risk for marginalisation. The project which is a 2-year pilot in Bara district in the Terai region in the south of Nepa
l aims to increase the enrollment of 3 to 8-year-old children with vision or hearing impairment in public preschools. The inhabitants of the district are poor and the number of children not attending school is considerable. The majority of pupils in Bara a
re boys and the project aims to promote girls' right to education.Grass-root level volunteers are educated to identify children with problems with vision or hearing. The children identified through screening are examined at eye + ear camps to diagnose cond
itions affecting sight and vision. It is estimated that most of these children have problems that can easily be cured with simple treatments. Children with a severe visual or hearing disability will undergo a 4-6 month rehabilitation to enable life outside
 home and attending school. Awareness raising campaigns regarding eye- and ear health as well as rights of disabled children will be organised among local inhabitants during the camps.This project is as a collaboration between a Nepalese NGO B.P. Eye Found
ation and PSR Finland. The collaboration was initiated from Nepal. Like the previous work of B.P. Eye Foundation this project aims to screen for and promote treatment of disabling conditions as well as contribute to a change in the Nepalese view on the rig
hts of people with disabilities remove barriers and stigmas associated with disabilities and enable children with disabilities to become productive citizens. The Department of Education (DoED) Nepal has signed a Memorandum of Understanding on implementing 
these pilot protocols in other districts in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education Program into Lhomi Language in Nepal
rows.description:
Lhomi Preschool Program in Nepal is a liaison between Wycliffe Finland (WRK) and its partner Nepal Lhomi Society (NELHOS). The main output will be Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education program for the Lhomi.The status of children in Nepal is often 
very poor especially among indigenous people groups. Lhomi children are often discriminated. There is no preschools available and they must start primary school in Nepali language which is unknown for them. This will have a bad effect to their success in t
he school and finally it causes exclusion.Providing pre-school opportunity by their mother tongue this project will increase parity in the education levels of children in the target communities with other children in the country. Children's rights will be 
increased by providing them pre-school education that equip them and easy their transition to the first grade in Nepali instructed primary school. In doing so children will comprehend and learn faster they will stay in school and ultimately will increase t
heir self-development. This will be achieved through a well-founded Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education (MTB ECE) -program that will be developed in the target language.The project aims to reduce inequality and vulnerability of indigenous childre
n. The rights of girls disabled children and low cast children will be emphasized. Moreover gender equality reduction of inequality disability and climate sustainability will be part of the learning material.NELHOS is responsible of project implementation.
 As an organization they will gain capacity of mother tongue education as well as they become strong advocate of minority language groups and the rights of marginalized groups in Nepal.Main beneficiaries are the Lhomi language speaking children their paren
ts and the local education actors who benefit of increased capacity. The government supports mother tongue education and pre-schools but often they do not have resources to start schools in the remote areas.Key activities:- Influencing the attitudes of the
 stakeholders.- Producing MTB ECE program and material for it.- Training teachers.- Pilot classes.- Capacity building.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Computer Training for Employment to the Disabilities
rows.description: Ten unemployed visually impaired people will take a six-months long training course in computer technology. After the training they will be employed as computer teachers, computer operators or they will run computer cyber cafes.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Computer course for board members of DAN who use sign language and have low vision
rows.description: Teaching computer skills to deafblind people to enable them to communicate, access the internet, build organizational capacity and find employment.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Improving Gender Equality of BVI Women
rows.description:
BVI is an association of blind and visually impaired women. They want to advocate for the rights of BVI women by providing organizational management training, training on gender roles among spouses, personality development and mobility training to BVI wome
n, and strategy development training for the association

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Digitalizing accounting system for economic transparency by developing management skill for PWDs
rows.description:
This is a cooperative of PWDs that plans to digitalize their bookkeeping system and increase capacity. Three executives will learn how to use the sofware to be purchased, the group will purchase a computer, and 15 people will learn how to run the loans and
 collection scheme.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Developing the access of persons with intellectual disability in education and enhancing the rights of learning and entertainment
rows.description:
Parents with Intellectually Disabled Children (PWID) will start a six-months education project for their children. Five children will also learn how to make paper products like envelopes. The project will also develop accessible learning material for intel
lectually disabled children.

rows.title: Building Preparedness of Vulnerable Communities, in Nepal and Bangladesh, through inclusive disaster risk reduction initiatives.
rows.description: 4010 older women and men and their families (16,040 people) in Bangladesh and Nepal have built disaster prepared livelihoods in order to lessen the immediate impact of disasters.

rows.title: Strengthening Systems for Better Health (Swasthya ko lagi skakchyam Pranali)
rows.description: (Nepal):  The Strengthening Systems for Better Health (SSBH) activity improves access to sustainable, quality, equitable health services. SSBH supports the GON's new federal structures to promote uninterrupted health service delivery and continuously leverages global experience in health systems strengthening to accelerate improvements in health outcomes. USAID provides technical assistance to the Ministry of Health and Population (MOHP) and provincial and municipal governments to improve access to and use of quality maternal, newborn, and child health; nutrition; water, sanitation, and hygiene; and family planning services in 16 districts. SSBH supports provincial and local governments, and health workers (HWs) to plan, execute, and monitor the health emergency response, including for COVID-19 pandemic and natural disasters. Funding will expand the basic health service package, including nutrition services, to marginalized groups by increasing service delivery points, improving referral mechanisms, and supporting municipalities to improve public and private health services. USAID will train HWs and managers to strengthen the health sector's capacity to generate, analyze, and use real-time data for improved planning and management. Funds will focus on increasing the accountability of local health managers and HWs for quality service delivery by improving linkages among municipal governments, facilities, and patient populations; and leveraging the capacity of Health Facility Operation and Management Committees to make health services more responsive to local needs. .Abt Associates has extensive experience in Nepal and globally in expanding quality health services to vulnerable populations. The activity contributes to USAID/Nepal's CDCS DO3: Inclusive Health and Education Systems Strengthened and Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Goal Two: Increase America's prosperity by developing Nepal as a reliable economic partner..

rows.title: Reading for All: Disability Inclusion Education for Nepali Children
rows.description: (Nepal):  .While Nepal has achieved universal access to primary education, there remain pockets of marginalized groups - including children with disabilities (CwD) - that do not have access to education. The Reading for All (R4A) activity's objective is to improve reading outcomes for CwD and functional limitations in grades 1-3 in the 16 districts of USAID's Early Grade Reading Program (EGRP). FY 2020 funds will be used to implement an early disability and functional limitation screening tool for students; support the GON to develop a more detailed disability Education Management Information System (EMIS) to improve the quality of data on CwD; implement an education response system and provide necessary referrals; revise national teacher training modules to include support for CwD in early grade reading; and continue supporting teachers on instructional methods for teaching CwD to read.  ..Humanity & Inclusion, as a leading implementer on disability programming, is uniquely positioned to apply international learning to the Nepal context in this nascent sub-sector of disability-inclusive education. R4A's emphasis on supporting GON systems to provide equitable access to quality education ultimately contributes to improving key metrics of self-reliance, inclusive development, and government capacity. This activity contributes to Embassy Kathmandu's Integrated Country Strategy Mission Goal Two: Increase America's prosperity by developing Nepal as a reliable economic partner and Mission Objective 2.2, Nepal improves health and education outcomes to increase economic productivity... .

rows.title: Nepal Community Reconstruction Program (NCRP) IDIQ
rows.description: The objective of NCRP is to provide a framework for USAID/Nepal to address the catastrophic losses in public infrastructure caused by the earthquake and aftershocks.  This will help restore normalcy build resilience and move Nepal towards recovery by supporting the GONs efforts to rebuild and strengthen disaster resilient communities.  Through NCRP, USAID will construct turn-key public infrastructure facilities with a focus on, but not necessarily limited to schools and healthcare facilities.

rows.title: Nepal Community Reconstruction Project (NCRP): Task Order to reconstruct secondary schools in Kathmandu valley
rows.description: This Task Order includes all activities to complete construction of three secondary Kathmandu Valley schools and furnish essential items for the operation of the facilities. The schools include: i) Kanya Mandir Secondary School, Nhyokha, Kathmandu; ii) Shree Bhagawati Secondary School, Sankharapur, Kathmandu; and iii) Adarsha Secondary School, Sanothimi, Bhaktapur. The Contractor must provide all labor, supervision, material, equipment, and other resources as required to reconstruct and renovate secondary school facilities, in accordance with drawings, specifications and instructions provided under the Invitation for Bids (IFB). This Task Order includes a base bid item and an additive bid item. The base bid item is for new construction and all associated work for the construction of Kanya Mandir Secondary School and Shree Bhagawati Secondary School. The additive bid Item is for architectural, and seismic retrofitting and new construction of Adarsha Secondary School..

rows.title: Public Sector Executive Function
rows.description: Assist executive offices, ministries, and independent governmental bodies to operate more efficiently and effectively; and assist executive branch offices and citizens to incorporate democratic structures and principles into state building, their ongoing systems of governance, and public administration services. Assist with policies, procedures, and skill sets (including leadership and strategic management) to guide operations; implementation and enforcement of laws, regulations and policies; linkages between and among branches, levels and functions of government; international relations; financial management systems; civil service reforms; public/private partnerships; and working with citizens as customers of the government.

rows.title: Integrated Watershed Management Activity
rows.description: Integrated Watershed Management Activity, USAID/Nepal for the Social, Environmental and Economic Development (SEED) Integration Activity, a two-year Associate Award with the objective of improving water security through better coordination and integration of USAID/Nepal's Feed the Future, Environment and Resilience activities. SWP will support USAID/Nepal and SEED implementing partners working in the Rangun Khola and Lower Karnali watersheds to develop and implement integrated plans to improve water security and strategically use results and learning to inform programming around more effective water resources management..

rows.title: Program Design and Learning
rows.description: This program area covers components of program design and performance management and learning. This area supports assessment, special studies and analysis, strategic planning, program and project design, program monitoring (to include baseline studies and other data collection needs) and activities that support learning, knowledge transfer and adaptation of projects. Assessment includes the examination of the state of a country or sector context to inform project design but does not include evaluation of USG-funded activities (please see Program Area PO.3 for a definition of evaluation). Special studies or analysis could support strategic or project planning or include research for general learning that is not necessarily related to the performance of USG-funded activities. This program area may also include the preparation of strategic plans and other short-term programming tasks, assessment of the potential of information and communication technologies to enhance performance throughout the program cycle or dissemination of best practices and lessons learned.

rows.title: Social Services
rows.description: Assist special populations which may be vulnerable or at-risk on a temporary or chronic basis whose needs are not addressed under emergency humanitarian assistance or other programs. These include groups such as the disabled; orphans, children and at-risk youth; victims of trafficking; victims of gender-based violence; refugees, returnees, ethnic minorities, internally displaced or other socially excluded groups; the elderly; and female heads of household. Depending on circumstances, services may intend to protect groups; mitigate adverse conditions they face, or to remove barriers to help integrate them into society. Components may include measures to increase the capacity of local service and advocacy NGOs and/or professional social workers; to establish public/private service delivery partnerships as well as family and community focused service models; to establish effective referral networks; to develop appropriate service protocols and methods for screening prospective recipients; or to improve public understanding and sensitivity to the needs of the vulnerable. While services provided should not exclude victims of trafficking in persons, programs designed specifically for this group fall under the Peace and Security Objective under the Transnational Crime Area and the Trafficking in Persons and Migrant Smuggling Element.

rows.title: Feed the Future Nepal Integrated Pest Management (FTFNIPM)
rows.description: (Nepal):  USAID's Integrated Pest Management (IPM) activity will invest FY 2021 resources to increase farmers' adoption of IPM for vegetables, maize, rice, and lentils by promoting the use of an integrated package of technologies and practices to minimize the use of chemical pesticides. Endorsed by USAID and the Government of Nepal, IPM minimizes the use of chemical pesticides while effectively controlling pests and diseases to benefit farmers, consumers, and the environment. Through training and targeted technical assistance, it will build public and private sector stakeholders' capacities for science-based pest management and pesticide selection processes to reduce pests, including Fall Armyworm, pesticide risk, and conduct pesticide safety training of trainers. Using FY 2021 funds, this activity will partner with agro-dealers to conduct field trials and demonstrations of IPM technologies side-by-side with conventionally treated crops. It will closely collaborate with other Feed the Future activities to share methods and practices that have proven successful in managing pests using integrated methods. The activity will expand the scale of its interventions to improve technologies and practices with women and marginalized groups, and train and empower women scientists and plant health professionals. .Virginia Tech University was selected due its technical capacity to implement this intervention in partnership with its Nepal-based sub-awardees and with public and private sector stakeholders. This activity will utilize $50,000 of Adaptation - Indirect directives. It contributes to USAID/Nepal's CDCS DO2: Broad-Based and Inclusive Economic Growth Fostered, and Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Goal Two: Increase America's prosperity by developing Nepal as a reliable economic partner... .. .

rows.title: Private Sector Productivity
rows.description: Improve the capacity of businesses to integrate into domestic and international markets through increases in productivity and improvements in corporate governance and the development and application of modern technology and marketing practices. Enhance the capacity of businesses and business associations in terms of their corporate governance capabilities and their application of best practices and technologies to achieve increased production and marketing of products and services. It includes adoption of efficient production processes, improved labor productivity, environmentally-sound management of natural resources and the development of accounting and management systems that can help the company and industry group implement business, labor, product and process standards that enhance business competitiveness. Business capacity development programs may include activities that help men- and women-owned firms and associations respond to these international markets and create regional alliances.

rows.description: USAID Inclusive Livelihood is to empower women and men with disabilities to engage in resilient and sustainable employment.

rows.title: USAID Inclusive Livelihoods - Central
rows.description: USAID Inclusive Livelihoods  Central activity is being implemented in Kathmandu and Dhading District in central Nepal. This is a three-year fixed amount milestone-based USAID activity, with 12 milestones (summarized below) to be achieved by the end of the project on October 31, 2025. The overall objective is the economic empowerment of people with disabilities in both rural and urban areas of central Nepal, through the mobilization of people with disabilities, livelihoods training and to promoting inclusive employment at the national level. The activitys inclusive livelihoods component aims to provide people with disabilities and their households with the mutual support, experience, skills, and capacity that will bring them greater economic independence and resilience. Ten women-led self-help groups (SHGs) will be established in each of Gokarneshwor (an urban/peri-urban municipality in Kathmandu) and Thakre Rural Municipality in Dhading District, bringing together at least 180 people with disabilities and family careers in each area, who will be supported to set up small enterprises for income generation and saving. Through the activitys training component in Kathmandu valley, 224 young people with disabilities, mostly women, will gain access to vocational training designed to meet both their learning needs and the requirements of employers. By completing their training courses and through mentoring and work-related learning, 224 young adults will have improved prospects for meaningful employment. The activitys advocacy component aims to celebrate and multiply inclusive employment practices, to promote enabling environments and help tackle the legal, regulatory, and cultural barriers that hold people with disabilities back from full participation in economic life. rows.title: MEL Support
rows.description: The purpose of the five-year Learning for Development contract is to provide USAID/Nepal with technical and advisory services in planning, designing, conducting, and learning from the required monitoring.

rows.title: Private Sector Productivity
rows.description: Improve the capacity of businesses to integrate into domestic and international markets through increases in productivity and improvements in corporate governance and the development and application of modern technology and marketing practices. Enhance the capacity of businesses and business associations in terms of their corporate governance capabilities and their application of best practices and technologies to achieve increased production and marketing of products and services. It includes adoption of efficient production processes, improved labor productivity, environmentally-sound management of natural resources and the development of accounting and management systems that can help the company and industry group implement business, labor, product and process standards that enhance business competitiveness. Business capacity development programs may include activities that help men- and women-owned firms and associations respond to these international markets and create regional alliances.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Economic upliftment of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities and their parents through production and selling of spices
rows.description: An association of parents of people with intellectual disabilities will start producing spices. They will purchase machines and raw material, select the beneficiaries of the project, draft a marketing strategy and start the production.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Capacity building of persons with disability
rows.description:
The organization will organize a vocational training course for five women with disabilities. The other activities of the project are related to capacity building of the organization: formation of 4 Self Help Groups, leadership training, and proposal and r
eport writing training. The goal is to enable the self help groups to apply for local grants. The organization will also celebrate International Day of People with Disabilties on 3 December.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empowering Blind Persons in Morang District
rows.description:
Morang Association of the Blind (MAB) will provide organizational management training, and advocacy and lobbying skills training to their membership. MAB will also provide musical training and vocational training like mushroom production and goat rearing. 
The intention is for the trainees to apply for local government money to start working after training.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Inclusion and independence of persons with congenital deafblindness in Nepal
rows.description:
Congenitally deafblind people will be tought activites of daily living (personal hygiene, dressing, eating etc.), and basic living skills (interacting with other people). The group will produce video clips of deafblind people´s situation to raise awarenes
s about deafblindness. A workshop for concerned government authorities and and other DPOs will be organized. Nepal is due to submit a report on the implementation of UNCRPD, and the group will contribute to a shadow report by giving information about the s
ituation of deafblind people.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Dialogues for ensuring CPRD principles in the forthcoming Disability Bill in Nepal
rows.description:
The Office of Abilis Local Expert in Nepal wants to arrange a series of ten dialogues with nepalese parliamentarians and DPOs to ensure that the now tabled Nepal Disability Bill will include human rights of persons with disabilities, in line with the UNCRP
D.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Basic Computer Training to Blind Persons in Far-West Region
rows.description:
25 blind students will take a 75 days long basic computer training course to be able to utilize electronic learning material in their studies. 50 blind students will take a two-day course to learn how to use their Android devices to access internet, e-mail
 and electronic books.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Economic Empowerment of PWDs and Local Resource Mobilization
rows.description:
The applicant organization was established by Women with Disabilities. The project plan is to provide leadership training for 30 leaders of local Self-Help-Groups, conduct an awareness raising campaign by broadcasting in FM radio, and to provide vocational
 training (goat rearing for 10 women, wood carving for five men).

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Enterpreneurship development of Deaf Women and promotion of sign language
rows.description:
DAR is planning to implement four activities: 1) Training on doll making for hearing impaired women, 15 days 2) Collecting local signs to be added to the national sign language dictionary of Nepal) 3) Training on inclusive education to 25 Deaf leaders and 
parents of Deaf children, 3 days 4) Setting up a showroom for showcasing the dolls made by the Deaf women.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Support by the Finnish National Agency for Education to school sector development in Nepal
rows.description:
The Finnish National Agency for Education (EduFi) has supported education sector reform in Nepal since 2005. EduFi's (previously FNBE) support has strengthened Finland's capacity to participate in the monitoring and dialogue related to the comprehensive Sc
hool Sector Reform Program (SSDP). EduFi's expertize has contributed to the Finland's excellent visibility and position in Nepal's education sector. In 2017 FinEdu supports the Ministry of Education in Nepal in developing its curriculum teachers' guides an
d student counseling. In addition EduFi provides the MFA with expert support relating to the implementation of both Finnish funded interventions in the education sector in Nepal. In the School Sector Development Plan which Finland funds in the years 2016-2
020 EduFi provides expertize in monitoring and reviewing the achievement of the programme. In the TASS technical support program EduFi gives the MFA recommendations relating to the practical roll-out of the program activities on the school level. The total
 budget of this support is 60.000 eur.

rows.title: Suaahara II (Integrated Nutrition Program II)
rows.description: (Nepal):  Suahaara II focuses on the 1,000-day period between conception and a child's second birthday as a critical window to shaping a child's cognitive and physical development through improved nutrition. With FY 2021 funds, the activity will continue to reduce the prevalence of stunting, underweight, and wasting among children; improve household health, nutrition and water, sanitation and hygiene behaviors and practices; increase the use of quality health services; increase the consumption of diverse and nutritious foods by women and their families; and support counseling and behavior change communication on family planning in mothers' groups. Suaahara II will work with the GON in 389 of the 753 municipalities in Nepal using evidence-based, multi-sectoral nutrition interventions at the policy, community, and household levels. Suaahara II will improve health and nutrition behaviors and reduce the impact of health emergencies such as COVID-19 on health and nutrition by strengthening community health systems. This will include training health facility staff on the promotion of exclusive breastfeeding, infant and young child feeding, maternal and newborn care, integrated management of acute malnutrition and childhood illness, and maternal and adolescent nutrition. In addition, it will promote essential hygiene actions (e.g., safe drinking water, disposal of feces), healthy timing and spacing of pregnancy, and provide information on family planning methods and service availability..This activity will utilize $50,000 of the $5 million Water earmark..Helen Keller International has vast experience implementing integrated nutrition interventions essential to achieve the goals of the activity. Improving nutritional health ensures a healthy and productive workforce in support of Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Goal Two: Increase America's prosperity by developing Nepal as a reliable economic partner..

rows.title: Civil Society: Mutual Accountability Project
rows.description: (Nepal):  The Civil Society: Mutual Accountability Project (CS:MAP) supports civil society organizations (CSOs), non-profit organizations, non-government media, and member-based organizations to foster a more legitimate, accountable, and resilient Nepali civil society to advance citizen demands. The devolution of budgetary and legal powers to local government underscores the importance of continuous monitoring and engagement by the media, non-profit organizations, and CSOs to engage in productive dialogues with the GON to increase its responsiveness to citizen needs. FY 2020 resources will build the capacity of key institutions to develop governance standards that adhere to international norms; foster engagement with the GON to help enact and implement policies that meet public demands; provide training on data-driven research and advocacy; and promote social accountability, crisis response mechanisms, information, and communication technology tools. CS:MAP also supports the GON's human rights oversight institutions to enhance accountability on human rights issues and train human right defenders. COVID-19 interventions will focus on citizen advocacy, countering disinformation, and engagement with the government to improve service delivery..FHI 360 has worked for over two decades in Nepal to improve public awareness, promote the use of social accountability, and advance the institutional sustainability of CSOs and the media. NS is critical to achieving U.S. objectives in the Integrated Country Strategy (ICS) and the Indo-Pacific Strategy. CS:MAP supports Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Mission Goal One: Promote American Security by supporting a stable, secure, democratic, and sovereign Nepal and Mission Objective 1.3, Nepal successfully transitions to a more stable, inclusive, and effective federal democracy based on international norms and free from malign influence. .

rows.title: Hariyo Ban II (Green Forests II) Program
rows.description: (Nepal):  USAID's Hariyo Ban II activity reduces threats to biological diversity and the impacts of climate change within two conservation landscapes: Terai Arc Landscape (TAL) and Chitwan Annapurna Landscape (CHAL) covering 40 percent of Nepal. It applies an integrated landscape perspective to biodiversity conservation and sustainable forests and watersheds management in partnership with community groups in four biodiversity corridors in TAL and three sub-river basins in CHAL that complement policy support at federal, provincial, and local levels. .With FY 2019 funds, Hariyo Ban II will provide technical assistance to improve the management of 200,000 hectares of biodiverse forests and buffer zones surrounding national parks. It will support conservation and enterprise activities benefiting over 10,000 people. USAID will invest in risk reduction activities that build resilience, benefiting about 30,000 people, and will integrate gender equity and social inclusion considerations into the policies of 100 forest user groups. The activity will safeguard biological diversity, combat wildlife crime, and promote judicious use of natural resources, thereby preserving Nepal's long-term economic prospects. .USAID/Nepal awarded the activity to World Wide Fund for Nature because of its technical expertise in managing natural resources development activities and its long established working relationships with host-country government and non-government stakeholders. The activity builds self-reliance and advances key objectives of the U.S. Government's Indo-Pacific Strategy by addressing threats to biodiversity and supporting resilience-building measures. Hariyo Ban II contributes to Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Goal Two: Increase America's prosperity by developing Nepal as a reliable economic partner, and Mission Objective 2.3, Nepal establishes environmentally sustainable practices and demonstrates increased capacity to safeguard natural resources..

rows.title: Hamro Samman (HS)
rows.description: (Nepal):  USAID's works to Combat Trafficking in Persons (CTIP) through the Hamro Samman (HS) activity that partners with the GON, civil society, and private sector partners to strengthen key relationships among stakeholders. The lack of coordination among the GON, civil society, and private sector, combined with the dearth of effective data collection and sharing among stakeholders continues to make Nepal an attractive environment for traffickers, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic. HS will use $1,500,000 of FY 2021 CTIP-earmarked funds and $100,000 of the Women, Peace and Security directive to: strengthen an integrated data collection system at the national and sub-national levels; provide technical assistance to national and local CTIP committees and help CSOs with data collection, analysis, and communication; facilitate information sharing among CSOs, government, and law enforcement; form private sector partnerships to expand access to quality services for trafficking survivors and at-risk groups; strengthen CTIP advocacy plans; provide shelter and counselling services for the reintegration of trafficking victims; and expand access to legal resources for survivors. In response to COVID-19, HS will equip shelters with materials needed for infection prevention and control to protect the resident survivors and other shelter staff from infection. .Winrock International is best placed to meet USAID's goals in CTIP as it has 30 years of experience working with governments to leverage CTIP data through evidence-based policy and survivor services.  By strengthening national and local level CTIP capacity, Nepal will be better able to disrupt criminal networks, reduce rights violations, and decrease regional and U.S. bound trafficking and human smuggling. HS supports Embassy Kathmandu's ICS Goal One: Promote American Security by supporting a stable, secure, democratic and sovereign Nepal..
